facts.t: Thomas Jefferson is inaugurated as the third U.S. president, the first inauguration held in Washington, D.C.
Jefferson breaks precedent by walking to his inauguration instead of arriving in a carriage.
Tripoli declares war on the United States, igniting the First Barbary War over piracy tribute demands.
The lame-duck Judiciary Act of 1801 lets outgoing President Adams appoint last-minute midnight judges.
Eli Whitney stages a famous demonstration of muskets he presents as built from interchangeable parts for U.S. officials.
Congress establishes the United States Military Academy at West Point.
President Jefferson signs the repeal of the federal whiskey excise tax that had sparked the Whiskey Rebellion.
The Ohio Enabling Act passes Congress, setting the stage for Ohio statehood.
Georgia cedes its vast western land claims to the federal government.
West Point holds its first classes, admitting a small handful of cadets to the new military academy.
The United States completes the Louisiana Purchase from France, doubling the nation's size.
Ohio becomes the 17th state of the Union.
The Supreme Court's Marbury v. Madison ruling establishes the principle of judicial review.
Congress funds the Lewis and Clark Expedition to explore the newly acquired western lands.
Young naturalist John James Audubon arrives in America and soon conducts one of the first bird-banding experiments in North America.
Vice President Aaron Burr fatally wounds Alexander Hamilton in their famous duel in Weehawken, New Jersey.
The 12th Amendment is ratified, reforming how the president and vice president are elected.
The Lewis and Clark Expedition departs from near St. Louis to explore the Louisiana Territory.
The Corps of Discovery carries whiskey as part of its rations, doled out to boost morale on hard days.
Thomas Jefferson wins reelection in a landslide over Federalist Charles Cotesworth Pinckney.
The Lewis and Clark Expedition reaches the Pacific Ocean.
U.S. Marines and mercenaries storm Derna, Tripoli, inspiring the line to the shores of Tripoli in the Marines Hymn.
The First Barbary War ends with a treaty securing safer passage for American ships.
Congress organizes the Michigan Territory out of the old Northwest Territory.
On his 31st birthday during the expedition, Meriwether Lewis writes a journal entry vowing to be more useful to mankind.
The Lewis and Clark Expedition returns triumphantly to St. Louis after more than two years.
Army officer Zebulon Pike sights the Colorado peak later named Pikes Peak in his honor.
Aaron Burr sets off down the Ohio River on a mysterious expedition that later sparks treason charges.
Congress funds construction of the National Road, the first major federally built highway.
A total solar eclipse sweeps across the U.S., drawing amazed crowds of spectators.
The British warship HMS Leopard attacks the USS Chesapeake, inflaming tensions that lead toward the War of 1812.
Robert Fulton's steamboat completes its historic voyage up the Hudson River.
Congress passes the Embargo Act, banning American ships from trading with foreign ports.
Aaron Burr stands trial for treason and is acquitted for lack of evidence of an overt act.
Meriwether Lewis is appointed governor of the Louisiana Territory following his famous expedition.
The federal ban on importing enslaved people from Africa takes effect, though smuggling continues.
James Madison wins the presidential election, succeeding Thomas Jefferson.
The Embargo Act devastates New England's shipping trade, fueling smuggling along the Canadian border.
John Jacob Astor founds the American Fur Company, which will make him one of the richest men in America.
Madison is picked by a Democratic-Republican congressional nominating caucus, the informal system used to choose early nominees.
James Madison is inaugurated as the fourth U.S. president.
Abraham Lincoln is born in a log cabin in Kentucky.
Explorer Meriwether Lewis dies under mysterious circumstances at a remote Tennessee inn along the Natchez Trace.
Congress replaces the unpopular Embargo Act with the Non-Intercourse Act, reopening trade with all nations except Britain and France.
Washington Irving publishes his comic A History of New York under the pen name Diedrich Knickerbocker.
American settlers in Spanish West Florida revolt and briefly declare the independent Republic of West Florida.
The Supreme Court's Fletcher v. Peck decision marks the first time it strikes down a state law as unconstitutional.
Congress passes Macon's Bill Number 2, reopening trade and dangling incentives to end British and French shipping restrictions.
John Jacob Astor's Pacific Fur Company is founded, setting the stage for the settlement of Astoria, Oregon.
The 1810 census counts the U.S. population at just over 7.2 million people.
William Henry Harrison's forces defeat Tecumseh's confederacy at the Battle of Tippecanoe.
A series of massive New Madrid earthquakes begins, among the most powerful ever recorded in North America.
Construction begins on the Cumberland Road, the first major federally funded highway.
The charter of the First Bank of the United States expires after Congress declines to renew it.
The steamboat New Orleans sets off from Pittsburgh, becoming the first steam-powered vessel to travel the Mississippi.
The United States declares war on Britain, beginning the War of 1812.
Louisiana becomes the 18th U.S. state.
The USS Constitution earns the nickname Old Ironsides after defeating HMS Guerriere in a famous naval battle.
Massachusetts Governor Elbridge Gerry signs a redistricting bill so oddly shaped it gives rise to the term gerrymander.
The most powerful of the New Madrid earthquakes strikes, felt as far away as Washington, D.C.
Commodore Oliver Hazard Perry wins the Battle of Lake Erie, reporting we have met the enemy and they are ours.
Shawnee leader Tecumseh is killed at the Battle of the Thames, ending his pan-Indian confederacy.
American forces burn York, the Upper Canada capital and present-day Toronto, foreshadowing British retaliation on Washington.
A Troy, New York meatpacker named Samuel Wilson, who supplied army barrels stamped U.S., becomes linked to the Uncle Sam legend.
Baltimore's swift privateer ships, the Baltimore Clippers, become feared raiders of British merchant shipping.
British troops burn Washington, D.C., torching the White House and the Capitol.
Before torching the White House, British officers reportedly sit down to eat the dinner and drink the wine left behind after the Madisons fled.
Francis Scott Key writes the poem that becomes The Star-Spangled Banner after watching the bombardment of Fort McHenry.
The Treaty of Ghent is signed, formally ending the War of 1812.
New England Federalists convene the Hartford Convention to air grievances against the war, damaging their party's reputation.
Andrew Jackson leads American forces to a decisive victory at the Battle of New Orleans, fought after the war had technically ended.
Pirate and smuggler Jean Lafitte aids Jackson's defense of New Orleans in exchange for a pardon.
The United States fights the brief Second Barbary War, forcing Algiers to end its demands for tribute.
News of the Treaty of Ghent finally reaches the U.S., confirming peace with Britain weeks after the New Orleans battle.
Captain Henry Miller Shreve pilots the steamboat Enterprise upstream from New Orleans, proving steam travel against the current is possible.
The Year Without a Summer brings snow and killing frosts to New England in June, ruining crops after the Tambora eruption.
Indiana becomes the 19th U.S. state.
Congress charters the Second Bank of the United States.
James Monroe is elected president in a landslide as the Federalist Party fades.
The American Colonization Society is founded to resettle free Black Americans in West Africa.
Construction begins on the Erie Canal, a project that will transform trade between the Great Lakes and the Atlantic.
James Monroe is inaugurated, ushering in the so-called Era of Good Feelings.
Mississippi becomes the 20th U.S. state.
The First Seminole War erupts in Florida as U.S. forces pursue Seminoles across the Spanish border.
New York brokers formally organize under a new constitution, forerunner of the New York Stock Exchange.
Illinois becomes the 21st U.S. state.
Andrew Jackson invades Spanish Florida and executes two British nationals, sparking an international incident.
Congress passes the Flag Act of 1818, standardizing the U.S. flag at 13 stripes with one star added per state.
A credit contraction takes hold that will spiral into the Panic of 1819, the nation's first major financial crisis.
The steamboat Walk-in-the-Water becomes the first steam-powered vessel on the Great Lakes.
The Panic of 1819 hits, becoming the young nation's first major peacetime financial crisis.
Spain cedes Florida to the United States under the Adams-Onis Treaty.
The Supreme Court's McCulloch v. Maryland ruling affirms federal power, declaring the power to tax involves the power to destroy.
Alabama becomes the 22nd U.S. state.
The SS Savannah becomes the first steamship to cross the Atlantic Ocean, though it relies mostly on sail.
Congress passes the Missouri Compromise, admitting Maine as a free state and Missouri as a slave state while banning slavery north of 36 30.
Maine becomes the 23rd U.S. state after splitting off from Massachusetts.
The Land Act of 1820 lowers the price of federal land and ends the government's credit-purchase system.
James Monroe wins reelection essentially unopposed, receiving all but one electoral vote.
Frontier legend Daniel Boone dies in Missouri at around 85 years old.
Missouri is admitted as the 24th state after a contentious second compromise over free Black citizens' rights.
Spain's formal transfer of Florida to the United States is completed, and Andrew Jackson becomes its first military governor.
Emma Willard opens the Troy Female Seminary in New York, one of the first schools to give women a rigorous academic education.
Boston English High School opens as the first public high school in the United States.
The Saturday Evening Post begins publication in Philadelphia.
A planned slave rebellion led by Denmark Vesey is uncovered in Charleston, South Carolina, resulting in dozens of executions.
The first settlers backed by the American Colonization Society found the West African colony that becomes Liberia's capital, Monrovia.
Trader William Becknell blazes a wagon route to Santa Fe, establishing what becomes known as the Santa Fe Trail.
Congress abolishes the federal government's network of Indian trading posts, handing the fur trade fully to private companies.
Southern state militias tighten patrols and restrictions on free Black communities in the wake of the Vesey conspiracy.
President Monroe issues the Monroe Doctrine, warning European powers against further colonization in the Americas.
A Visit from St. Nicholas, better known as Twas the Night Before Christmas, is first published anonymously.
Explorer Stephen Long's expedition report labels the Great Plains the Great American Desert, discouraging westward settlement for decades.
A long middle stretch of the Erie Canal opens, letting boats travel far across New York years before the full canal is completed.
Fur trapper Hugh Glass is mauled by a grizzly bear and left for dead, then crawls hundreds of miles to survival.
The Marquis de Lafayette returns to the United States for a triumphant farewell tour, greeted by adoring crowds in every state.
The presidential election ends without an electoral majority, throwing the contest between Jackson, Adams, and others into the House.
The Supreme Court's Gibbons v. Ogden decision strikes down a steamboat monopoly, affirming federal authority over interstate commerce.
During his tour, Lafayette visits an aging Thomas Jefferson at Monticello for an emotional reunion of Revolutionary War comrades.
Secretary of War John C. Calhoun establishes the Bureau of Indian Affairs within the War Department.
The Erie Canal fully opens, connecting the Great Lakes to the Atlantic and transforming American trade.
John Quincy Adams is inaugurated after the House resolves the deadlocked election, prompting Jackson supporters to cry corrupt bargain.
Governor DeWitt Clinton ceremonially pours a keg of Lake Erie water into the Atlantic Ocean to mark the canal's completion.
Utopian reformer Robert Owen founds the experimental communal settlement of New Harmony, Indiana.
Cheap whiskey floods canal-side towns along the new Erie Canal, alarming the growing temperance movement.
